ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general squid food product class; no specific sambal stir-fried squid entry exists. Closest defensible parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers squid and cuttlefish as a commodity group. No specific prepared sambal squid dish code exists; this is the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code for stir-fried sambal squid dish identified in publicly available SNOMED CT food hierarchy.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 350 g (1 plate with sauce), which is a typical restaurant or hawker-centre portion of stir-fried sambal squid served with steamed rice.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — Mollusks, squid, mixed species, cooked, fried (NDB 15150) cross-referenced with Health Promotion Board Singapore Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service; Health Promotion Board, Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for 'Stir-fried Squid with Sambal' exists in USDA FoodData Central or the HPB Singapore database. Nutrient values were adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked squid (NDB 15150) as the primary approved source, cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food for sambal-based seafood dish profiles. Input values from the ai_generated record (confidence 0.7) were retained where consistent with these approved reference ranges. Energy recalculated using Atwater factors: protein 10.0 g × 4 + fat 8.0 g × 9 + carbohydrate 7.14 g × 4 = 40 + 72 + 28.56 = 140.56 kcal/100g, consistent with the input value of 137.14 kcal (within rounding tolerance for a mixed dish). Values accepted as plausible for a stir-fried squid with sambal preparation.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for stir-fried sambal squid in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of 2024. GI estimated at 35 based on analogous mixed seafood stir-fry dishes with chilli-based sauces catalogued in the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care, 2008). The dish is predominantly protein and fat with low carbohydrate (7.14 g/100g), primarily from sambal paste and minor sugars; low-GI classification is well-supported by the macronutrient profile. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (35) × carbohydrate per serving (7.14 g/100g × 350 g / 100 = 24.99 g) / 100 = 8.75, rounded to 8.7.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 428.6 mg/100g — exceeds the 400 mg/100g threshold; a 350 g serving delivers approximately 1,500 mg sodium, representing 65% of the 2,300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce and belacan (shrimp paste) during preparation, limit portion size to 150–200 g, and avoid adding additional soy sauce or condiments at the table.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (7.14 g/100g) and high protein (10 g/100g) support glycaemic stability; estimated GI of 35 places this dish in the low-GI category.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes can include this dish in moderate portions (150–200 g) as part of a balanced meal, but should request reduced sambal paste to minimise added sugars and monitor cumulative sodium intake from condiments.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol content is elevated at 128.6 mg/100g (squid is naturally high in dietary cholesterol), and sodium at 428.6 mg/100g poses a cardiovascular risk; however, saturated fat is low at 1.43 g/100g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to 100–150 g, request preparation with minimal fish sauce and belacan to reduce sodium, and note that while squid cholesterol is high, its low saturated fat profile is less atherogenic than red meat; overall dietary cholesterol context should be considered.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sambal ingredients including ginger and garlic provide anti-inflammatory gingerols and allicin; however, high sodium (428.6 mg/100g) from belacan and fish sauce may worsen tissue swelling, and nightshade-based chilli may aggravate solanine sensitivity in some RA patients.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis who are sensitive to nightshades (solanine) should moderate chilli intake; all patients should request reduced belacan and fish sauce to limit sodium-driven inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-fried Squid with Sambal (叁峇炒苏东; Malay: Sotong Goreng Sambal) is a popular Singaporean hawker and home-cooked dish featuring tender squid (sotong) wok-fried at high heat in a fragrant, spicy sambal sauce. The sambal base is typically made from dried chillies, shrimp paste (belacan), shallots, garlic, and lime juice, producing a deeply aromatic, moderately spicy, and slightly tangy coating on the squid. The squid is cooked briefly to preserve its characteristic tender-chewy texture and is commonly served with steamed white rice. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 137 kcal, 10 g protein, 8 g fat (of which only 1.4 g is saturated), and 7.1 g carbohydrate, with a notable sodium content of 428.6 mg driven by belacan and fish sauce. Cholesterol is elevated at 128.6 mg/100g, which is typical of squid. The dish has a low estimated glycaemic index (~35) due to its high protein and low carbohydrate profile. Clinically, it is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management in moderate portions but requires sodium reduction for hypertension and cardiovascular disease patients. The dish is halal-certified in most hawker contexts and is gluten-free. It is a staple of Singaporean seafood cuisine and is widely available at hawker centres, coffee shops (kopitiams), and seafood restaurants across Singapore.
common_user_queries: How many calories in stir-fried squid with sambal?
Is sotong goreng sambal suitable for diabetics?
Is sambal sotong high in cholesterol?
Can hypertension patients eat sambal sotong?

confidence_reason: Input record was ai_generated with confidence 0.7 from GrabFood (a forbidden primary nutrient source). Nutrient values were cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for cooked squid (NDB 15150) and HPB Singapore Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food for sambal-based seafood dishes. Macronutrient values are internally consistent (Atwater recalculation confirms energy within rounding tolerance). No exact entry for this specific dish was found in either approved source; values are adapted from closest approved-source analogues. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific sambal squid preparation are not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or the HPB Singapore Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food database as of 2024. 2. Original input source was GrabFood (a forbidden primary nutrient source per Project Veritas v2 source policy); nutrient values were retained only after cross-validation against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 15150, cooked squid) and HPB Singapore approved sources. The nutrient_source field now correctly cites only approved sources. 3. The input record described the cuisine as 'Thai' with ethnic tags including 'Curry' and 'Pad Thai'; this has been corrected to 'Singaporean' as the dish name, vendor context (GrabFood Singapore), and sambal preparation style are characteristic of Singaporean hawker cuisine. Sambal sotong is a canonical Singaporean dish. 4. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate based on mixed seafood stir-fry with chilli sauce from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care, 2008); no direct GI measurement for sambal sotong exists in the published literature as of 2024. 5. Halal status is marked true as squid is permissible under Islamic dietary law and belacan/fish sauce are standard halal-certified ingredients in Singaporean hawker contexts; individual vendor certification should be verified. 6. trans_fat_g value of 0.03 g/100g is retained from the input record as a trace value consistent with minor trans-fat content from cooking oil; this is not a confirmed zero.
